personality_voice.book_baseline: In the books, Snape's voice is defined by layered, controlled menace: sardonic wit deployed with precision, deliberate cruelty toward Harry (point-deductions, taunting about James and Sirius, the detention with the old punishment cards), and a cold composure that only cracks under extreme provocation. He is verbose when it serves him — his rebuttal of Bellatrix's accusations in the Spinner's End chapter is a masterclass in controlled rhetorical dominance, methodically dismantling each charge with calm, slightly bored superiority. He uses irony as a weapon ('You astonish with your gifts, Potter'), speaks in long, structured sentences when arguing, and in clipped commands when dismissing. His cruelty is specific and personal: he targets Harry's father, his arrogance, his mediocrity. He also shows rare moments of raw emotion — his scream of 'DON'T CALL ME COWARD' and his anguished face when Harry uses his own spells against him reveal a man whose composure is a performance over deep wounds. His classroom persona — the loving caress in his voice when describing the Dark Arts — signals genuine complexity beneath the surface.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the sardonic register well in isolated moments: 'You astonish with your gifts, Potter, gifts mere mortals could only dream of possessing' (Film Scene 4) is a direct lift of his book voice. His controlled composure at the Unbreakable Vow, his clipped dismissal of Bellatrix, and his cold fury on the grounds ('Don't ever associate that word and my name again') are recognizable. However, the film strips away the extended rhetorical performances that define his voice in the books. The entire Spinner's End cross-examination — where Snape methodically, almost playfully, dismantles Bellatrix's accusations over many exchanges — is compressed into a brief summary monologue ('Over the years I have played my part well. So well I've deceived one of the greatest wizards of all time'). The classroom scenes establishing his pedagogical cruelty and his unsettling passion for the Dark Arts are absent from the film corpus. The detention scene with the punishment cards — one of the most revealing moments of his targeted personal cruelty — is absent. His 'Half-Blood Prince' reveal in the film is reduced to a single taunting line about Harry's mother's eyes and his father's dimness, losing the raw fury of the book confrontation.
personality_voice.difference: The film preserves the surface register of Snape's voice but removes the extended verbal performances that demonstrate its full range. The sardonic wit survives; the methodical rhetorical dominance, the classroom cruelty, the targeted personal attacks on Harry's lineage and the detention's deliberate psychological torture are largely absent. The emotional crack — 'DON'T CALL ME COWARD' — is replaced by a colder, more controlled rebuke. The loving caress when describing the Dark Arts is absent entirely from the film corpus.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality is recognizable — sardonic, controlled, menacing — but important secondary traits are reduced or absent: the extended rhetorical dominance, the pedagogical cruelty, the specific targeting of Harry's father, and the emotional volatility beneath the composure. This is a meaningful simplification that warrants a penalty into the 14-17 range.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: In the books, Snape serves multiple simultaneous narrative functions across HBP: (1) active antagonist to Harry in the classroom and through detentions; (2) ambiguous protector of Draco, initiating contact, pressing Draco repeatedly, and making the Unbreakable Vow; (3) the figure whose true allegiance drives the central mystery; (4) the agent who kills Dumbledore as the culmination of a plan; (5) the revealer of his own identity as the Half-Blood Prince, which recontextualizes the entire year. He also functions as a plot driver through the Occlumency/Legilimency dynamic, the detention scenes, and his argument with Dumbledore overheard by Hagrid. His agency is consistently active: he initiates, confronts, decides, and acts.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film preserves his role as Dumbledore's killer and the Half-Blood Prince reveal. His Unbreakable Vow and his attempt to protect Draco are present. However, several active agency functions are removed or reduced: the classroom antagonism (no DADA teaching scenes in the film corpus), the detention scenes, the extended pursuit of Draco (the film's corridor scene is compressed and Draco rebuffs him more decisively), and the Hagrid-overheard argument. In the tower scene, the film adds a significant agency shift: Snape silences Harry with a finger to his lips before ascending, positioning him as a knowing orchestrator — this is a film invention that actually increases his apparent agency in one moment, but it also removes the ambiguity that drives the book's mystery. His flight from Hogwarts is compressed; the book's extended chase where he deflects every curse while taunting Harry about his father and his spells is present in abbreviated form.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The classroom antagonist function is absent from the film corpus. The active pursuit of Draco across the term is compressed to a single corridor scene. The Hagrid-overheard argument (establishing Snape's resistance to Dumbledore's plan) is present in the film but relocated to the Astronomy Tower and reframed. The film adds the 'finger to lips' moment, which transfers some narrative orchestration to Snape that the book does not give him explicitly at that point.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The core narrative functions — killer of Dumbledore, Half-Blood Prince, Unbreakable Vow maker — are preserved. But the classroom antagonist role and the sustained active pursuit of Draco are significantly reduced. The film's Snape is less continuously present as an active force across the story. This warrants a penalty into the 10-13 range.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Snape's motivations in HBP are layered and only partially visible: he is bound by the Unbreakable Vow to protect Draco; he is operating under Dumbledore's orders to kill him when necessary; he is protecting his cover as a Death Eater; and beneath all of this, his remorse over Lily's death (revealed at the end of the book through Dumbledore's account) is the deepest motivating force. His internal conflict is acute: he has agreed to kill the man who gave him redemption, he is trying to protect a boy he may not particularly like, and he must maintain two incompatible loyalties simultaneously. The argument with Dumbledore overheard by Hagrid — 'Snape was feeling a bit overworked... didn't want to do it anymore' — hints at the cost of this position.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film preserves the Unbreakable Vow motivation and the surface of his dual-loyalty conflict. The Astronomy Tower argument ('Have you ever considered that you ask too much? That you don't want to do this anymore?') directly conveys his resistance to the plan. His words at the tower — 'I gave my word. I made a vow' — suggest the Vow as motivation for the killing, which is ambiguous: in the books, the Vow binds him to protect Draco and complete the task if Draco fails, but Dumbledore's plan is the deeper reason. The film collapses these into a single ambiguous statement that could be read as the Vow alone driving the killing, obscuring the Dumbledore-ordered dimension. The remorse over Lily — the deepest motivational layer — is entirely absent from the film corpus (it belongs to Deathly Hallows).
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film preserves the surface motivations (Vow, dual loyalty, resistance to the plan) but the Dumbledore-ordered dimension of the killing is less clear. The deepest motivational layer (Lily) is absent from this film's corpus, which is appropriate since it belongs to the next film. However, the film's framing of 'I gave my word, I made a vow' at the moment of killing ambiguously suggests the Vow — rather than Dumbledore's plan — as the primary driver, which is a meaningful distortion.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: The motivations are partially present but the most important one — that Dumbledore ordered the killing as part of a plan — is underexplained in the film, and the Vow framing at the killing moment creates a misleading impression. This is a meaningful flattening that warrants a penalty into the 8-10 range.
motivations_internal_conflict.evidence_status: Directly evidenced, with reasonable inference for the Dumbledore-plan dimension

character_arc.book_baseline: Snape's arc in HBP moves from ambiguous antagonist to apparent traitor, with the revelation that he is the Half-Blood Prince adding a retrospective layer to everything. The arc's key steps are: (1) his appointment to DADA, which he has wanted for years; (2) his sustained attempt to protect Draco while maintaining cover; (3) his argument with Dumbledore suggesting he is being pushed to a breaking point; (4) the killing of Dumbledore, which appears to confirm his treachery; (5) the Half-Blood Prince reveal, which recontextualizes his relationship to Harry all year. The arc ends on apparent betrayal, with the full redemptive arc deferred to Deathly Hallows. Within HBP alone, the arc is the movement from 'trusted but suspicious' to 'confirmed traitor' — a false arc that the series will later invert.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film preserves the broad shape: DADA appointment, Unbreakable Vow, killing of Dumbledore, Half-Blood Prince reveal, flight. However, several key steps are compressed or absent: the sustained classroom presence that makes his DADA appointment meaningful is absent from the film corpus; the Draco-protection arc across the term is compressed to one scene; the Hagrid-overheard argument is relocated and reframed. The Half-Blood Prince reveal in the film is a single line rather than the extended confrontation of the book. The arc's emotional texture — the year-long accumulation of suspicion, the classroom cruelty, the detentions — is largely absent.
character_arc.difference: The arc's endpoints are preserved but the intermediate steps that give it weight are compressed or missing. The DADA appointment's significance (Snape finally getting what he wanted) is not developed. The sustained Draco-protection effort across the term is reduced. The arc is present in skeleton form but lacks the flesh that makes it meaningful.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is incomplete and missing important steps. The beginning (DADA appointment as meaningful milestone) and the middle (sustained classroom presence, detentions, Draco pursuit) are significantly compressed. The ending is preserved. This warrants a score in the 8-10 range.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Three relationships define Snape in HBP: (1) Snape/Harry — active antagonism, classroom cruelty, detentions, the Half-Blood Prince dynamic where Harry unknowingly uses Snape's own spells and knowledge, culminating in the confrontation on the grounds; (2) Snape/Dumbledore — a relationship of mutual obligation, trust, and growing strain, with Snape resisting the plan and Dumbledore holding him to it; (3) Snape/Draco — a protective relationship Draco resents, with Snape repeatedly trying to help a boy who refuses his help, bound by the Vow and by his promise to Narcissa. The Snape/Narcissa relationship (Spinner's End) establishes his capacity for something like loyalty or compassion toward those he has known.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: Snape/Harry: The antagonism is present but reduced — no classroom scenes, no detentions, no Half-Blood Prince dynamic playing out across the year. The grounds confrontation is present but abbreviated. The film adds the 'finger to lips' moment which creates a different dynamic — Snape as knowing protector of Harry in that moment — which has no book basis. Snape/Dumbledore: The Astronomy Tower argument is present and captures the strain, but the year-long texture of their relationship is absent. Snape/Draco: The corridor scene is present but compressed; the book's sustained pursuit across the term (repeated summons, Draco's avoidance, Snape's frustration) is reduced to a single exchange. Snape/Narcissa: The Spinner's End scene is significantly compressed, losing the extended cross-examination by Bellatrix that reveals Snape's relationship to the Death Eater world.
key_relationships.difference: All three key relationships are present but substantially compressed. The Snape/Harry relationship loses the year-long classroom dynamic that is its primary texture. The Snape/Draco relationship loses the sustained pursuit. The Snape/Dumbledore relationship loses the year-long context. The film's 'finger to lips' moment invents a protective dynamic toward Harry that does not exist in the books at this point.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The emotional dynamics of all three key relationships are altered or weakened by compression. The invented 'finger to lips' moment changes the Snape/Harry dynamic in a way that softens the antagonism and adds a false protective note. This warrants a score in the 5-6 range.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Snape's complexity in HBP rests on several pillars: (1) the extended Spinner's End scene, where he outmaneuvers Bellatrix rhetorically while concealing his true allegiance from everyone including the reader; (2) the classroom scenes, where his passion for the Dark Arts and his targeted cruelty toward Harry coexist with genuine pedagogical insight (Hermione notes his DADA speech sounds like Harry's own description of facing Voldemort); (3) the detentions, where his cruelty is personal and deliberate — using James and Sirius's punishment records to torment Harry; (4) the Sectumsempra bathroom scene, where he heals Draco and then methodically investigates Harry, using Legilimency and the 'Roonil Wazlib' trap; (5) the Half-Blood Prince identity, which reveals that the 'helpful' annotations Harry has relied on all year came from Snape himself — a profound irony; (6) his argument with Dumbledore, suggesting he is being pushed beyond what he agreed to; (7) his behavior on the grounds — deflecting every curse while refusing to fight back, taunting Harry about his father, and finally revealing himself — which is simultaneously cruel, controlled, and anguished.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the Spinner's End scene in compressed form (losing the extended Bellatrix cross-examination), the Unbreakable Vow, the tower killing, the grounds confrontation (abbreviated), and the Half-Blood Prince reveal (reduced to one line). The bathroom scene, the detentions, the classroom scenes, the 'Roonil Wazlib' trap, and the extended rhetorical performances are absent from the film corpus. The nuance of Hermione's observation — that Snape's DADA speech sounds like Harry's own wisdom — is lost. The irony of Harry relying on Snape's annotations all year is present in the reveal but not developed across the film.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The film loses most of the material that makes Snape complex in HBP: the extended rhetorical dominance at Spinner's End, the classroom passion for the Dark Arts, the targeted personal cruelty of the detentions, the Sectumsempra/bathroom scene's cat-and-mouse dynamic, and the year-long irony of Harry unknowingly using Snape's own knowledge. What remains is the skeleton of his role without the flesh of his complexity.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The loss of the classroom scenes, detentions, bathroom scene, and extended Spinner's End cross-examination removes the material that makes Snape more than a plot function in HBP. The character is recognizable but substantially flattened. This warrants a score in the 8-10 range.

lost_or_transferred_material: Extended Spinner's End cross-examination: Snape's methodical, rhetorically dominant rebuttal of Bellatrix's accusations across many exchanges — compressed to a brief summary monologue in the film
DADA classroom scenes: Snape's unsettling passion for the Dark Arts ('a loving caress in his voice'), his targeted cruelty toward Harry and Ron, his dismissal of Hermione's correct answers
Detention scenes: Snape's deliberate use of James and Sirius's punishment records to torment Harry, his pointed remarks about Quidditch and 'poor Gryffindor'
Sectumsempra bathroom scene: Snape healing Draco, then methodically investigating Harry via Legilimency and the 'Roonil Wazlib' trap
Year-long Half-Blood Prince irony: Harry unknowingly relying on Snape's own annotations and knowledge across the entire year — present only as a single reveal line in the film
Snape's comment on Tonks's Patronus ('The new one looks weak') — a moment of gratuitous cruelty revealing his awareness of others' emotional states
Snape's 'DON'T CALL ME COWARD' emotional breakdown on the grounds — replaced by a colder, more controlled rebuke in the film
Snape's specific taunts about James on the grounds ('Your father would never attack me unless it was four on one') — absent from the film corpus
The 'Roonil Wazlib' interrogation scene — entirely absent
Hagrid-overheard argument relocated and reframed: in the books it is reported secondhand and ambiguous; in the film it is shown directly on the Astronomy Tower, removing the ambiguity
Film invention — 'finger to lips' moment: Snape silencing Harry before ascending the tower, positioning him as a knowing orchestrator protecting Harry — has no book basis and softens the antagonism
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if a core book function, decision, or act of agency is transferred to another character): The 'finger to lips' moment invents a protective function toward Harry not present in the books. Applied — score kept below 75.

Cap 1 (score above 85 if films remove a major part of complexity, agency, contradiction, or narrative function): The removal of classroom scenes, detentions, bathroom scene, and extended Spinner's End cross-examination removes major complexity. Applied — score kept well below 85.
key_observations: The film's Snape is recognizable in outline — sardonic, controlled, the killer of Dumbledore, the Half-Blood Prince — but the material that makes him genuinely complex in HBP is largely absent. The books' Snape is defined by sustained, active presence: the classroom cruelty, the detentions, the year-long irony of Harry using his annotations, the extended rhetorical performances, and the emotional volatility beneath the composure. The film reduces him to his plot functions while preserving his surface register. The most significant distortions are: (1) the compression of Spinner's End loses his rhetorical dominance; (2) the absence of classroom and detention scenes removes his active antagonist role; (3) the 'finger to lips' invention softens the Snape/Harry dynamic in a way that has no book basis; (4) the 'I gave my word, I made a vow' framing at the killing moment ambiguously suggests the Unbreakable Vow — rather than Dumbledore's plan — as the primary driver, which is a meaningful motivational distortion. The score of 63 reflects a character who is superficially faithful but missing most of what defines his book characterization in this installment.
